content.text: Team Retrospective Formats for Claude Delivery

Retrospectives for Claude delivery teams have a fundamentally different character from retrospectives for teams building deterministic systems. In a deterministic system, a regression almost always has a single proximate cause: a code change, a configuration change, or a dependency update. In a Claude integration, regressions are often emergent: a prompt that worked well for six months begins producing lower-quality responses because the distribution of user inputs drifted, the model's behaviour shifted between training runs, or a seemingly unrelated change to the context window structure changed the way the model weighted instructions. Finding the root cause requires examining multiple layers simultaneously — prompt text, model version, input distribution, evaluation methodology — and the retrospective format must reflect that complexity.

A team that runs standard agile retrospectives without adapting them for Claude-specific failure modes will produce action items that address symptoms rather than causes. 'Improve testing' is not an actionable item when the real cause is that the team's eval set does not cover the tail of the input distribution where quality degraded. 'Fix the prompt' is not actionable when the real cause is that the prompt was written for an older model version and the behaviour difference between versions was not caught because the team had no model upgrade fitness function. Claude delivery retrospectives need structured prompts, Claude-specific root-cause categories, and outcome tracking that goes beyond 'did we close the ticket?'

attrs.alt: Four retrospective formats for Claude delivery: Sprint Retro (biweekly), Quality Deep Dive (monthly), Architecture Retro (quarterly), Incident Retro (as-needed)

content.text: The four formats above cover different time horizons and different types of learning. Sprint retrospectives surface operational friction quickly enough to adjust within the current quarter. Quality deep dives identify systematic patterns in output quality that only become visible with a month of data. Architecture retrospectives ask whether the architectural decisions made 3–12 months ago have held up in practice. Incident retrospectives extract the maximum learning from high-severity events within days of the incident, when memory is fresh and the timeline is still reconstructible. Each format feeds the same improvement backlog, but they operate at different frequencies and require different preparation.
Sprint Retrospective Format

The sprint retrospective runs after every sprint and focuses on operational learnings that can be actioned within the next sprint. The format is adapted from the standard 'what went well / what went poorly / what to change' structure, with Claude-specific prompt categories that surface issues in the prompt development workflow, the evaluation process, and the integration with the broader engineering team.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import List, Optional


class SprintRetroCategory(Enum):
    PROMPT_QUALITY    = "prompt_quality"     # Prompt changes that caused unexpected behaviour
    EVAL_COVERAGE     = "eval_coverage"      # Gaps in the eval set that let regressions through
    INTEGRATION       = "integration"        # Issues at the boundary with other services
    MODEL_BEHAVIOUR   = "model_behaviour"    # Unexpected model outputs independent of prompt
    DEPLOYMENT        = "deployment"         # Issues in the path from development to production
    PROCESS           = "process"            # Team process friction


class RetroItemStatus(Enum):
    IDENTIFIED = "identified"
    ASSIGNED   = "assigned"
    CLOSED     = "closed"
    DEFERRED   = "deferred"


@dataclass
class SprintRetroItem:
    item_id: str
    sprint_id: str
    category: SprintRetroCategory
    quadrant: str   # "went_well" | "went_poorly" | "action"
    description: str
    owner: Optional[str] = None
    due_sprint: Optional[str] = None
    status: RetroItemStatus = RetroItemStatus.IDENTIFIED
    resolved_notes: str = ""

    def is_overdue(self, current_sprint: str) -> bool:
        """True if an action item has not been closed by its due sprint."""
        return (
            self.quadrant == "action"
            and self.due_sprint is not None
            and self.due_sprint < current_sprint
            and self.status != RetroItemStatus.CLOSED
        )


@dataclass
class SprintRetro:
    sprint_id: str
    facilitator: str
    attendees: List[str]
    items: List[SprintRetroItem] = field(default_factory=list)

    def action_items(self) -> List[SprintRetroItem]:
        return [i for i in self.items if i.quadrant == "action"]

    def unresolved_from_previous(self) -> List[SprintRetroItem]:
        """Action items that were deferred from a prior sprint."""
        return [
            i for i in self.items
            if i.status == RetroItemStatus.DEFERRED
        ]

    def category_distribution(self) -> dict:
        """Count items per category to identify systemic patterns."""
        counts = {cat.value: 0 for cat in SprintRetroCategory}
        for item in self.items:
            counts[item.category.value] += 1
        return counts

    def retro_summary(self) -> str:
        dist = self.category_distribution()
        actions = self.action_items()
        dominant = max(dist, key=lambda k: dist[k])
        return (
            f"Sprint {self.sprint_id} retro ({len(self.attendees)} attendees)\n"
            f"Total items: {len(self.items)} | Action items: {len(actions)}\n"
            f"Dominant category: {dominant} ({dist[dominant]} items)\n"
            f"Deferred from previous: {len(self.unresolved_from_previous())}"
        )
Quality Deep Dive Format

The quality deep dive runs monthly and takes a data-first approach: it begins with a prepared metrics report covering the previous month's eval scores, user feedback signals, and cost and latency trends, and then uses the data to direct root-cause investigation on the top regressions. The format is designed to prevent the retrospective from becoming a narrative exercise ('we had some issues with the summarisation prompt') and force the team to reason from evidence to cause.
Incident Retrospective Format for Claude Integrations

Incident retrospectives for Claude integrations run within five days of any P1 or P2 incident. The Claude-specific adaptation of the standard incident retro format adds a model and prompt attribution section: the team must determine whether the incident was caused by a change in the application code, a change in the prompt, a change in the underlying model's behaviour (including non-versioned drifts), or a combination. This attribution is necessary because the corrective action is different in each case — a code bug is fixed in the next deploy, a prompt regression is fixed in the prompt builder, and a model behaviour change may require a fitness function that gates on model version.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import List, Optional


class IncidentRootCauseCategory(Enum):
    APPLICATION_CODE  = "application_code"   # Bug in non-Claude code
    PROMPT_CHANGE     = "prompt_change"      # Prompt edit caused regression
    MODEL_BEHAVIOUR   = "model_behaviour"    # Model output changed without prompt change
    INTEGRATION       = "integration"        # Failure at API boundary
    INFRASTRUCTURE    = "infrastructure"     # Rate limits, timeouts, deployment
    PROCESS           = "process"            # Missing test, review gap, comms failure


@dataclass
class IncidentTimelineEntry:
    timestamp: str    # ISO-8601
    event: str
    discovered_by: str
    action_taken: str = ""


@dataclass
class ClaudeIncidentRetro:
    incident_id: str
    severity: str          # P1 | P2
    start_time: str        # ISO-8601
    resolution_time: str   # ISO-8601
    timeline: List[IncidentTimelineEntry] = field(default_factory=list)
    root_cause_category: Optional[IncidentRootCauseCategory] = None
    root_cause_description: str = ""
    # Claude-specific attribution
    prompt_version_at_incident: Optional[str] = None
    model_version_at_incident: Optional[str] = None
    prompt_changed_in_48h_before: bool = False
    model_version_changed_in_48h_before: bool = False
    # 5-whys
    why_chain: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    corrective_actions: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    preventive_actions: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    facilitator: str = ""
    attendees: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)

    def attribution_summary(self) -> str:
        lines = [
            f"Prompt version at incident: {self.prompt_version_at_incident or 'unknown'}",
            f"Prompt changed in prior 48h: {'YES — investigate' if self.prompt_changed_in_48h_before else 'no'}",
            f"Model version at incident: {self.model_version_at_incident or 'unknown'}",
            f"Model version changed in prior 48h: {'YES — investigate' if self.model_version_changed_in_48h_before else 'no'}",
        ]
        return "\n".join(lines)

    def retro_document(self) -> str:
        why_chain_str = "\n".join(
            f"  Why {i+1}: {why}" for i, why in enumerate(self.why_chain)
        )
        corrective_str = "\n".join(f"  - {a}" for a in self.corrective_actions)
        preventive_str = "\n".join(f"  - {a}" for a in self.preventive_actions)
        return (
            f"Incident {self.incident_id} ({self.severity}) Retrospective\n"
            f"Duration: {self.start_time} to {self.resolution_time}\n"
            f"\nAttribution:\n{self.attribution_summary()}\n"
            f"\nRoot cause ({self.root_cause_category.value if self.root_cause_category else 'TBD'}):\n"
            f"  {self.root_cause_description}\n"
            f"\n5-Whys:\n{why_chain_str}\n"
            f"\nCorrective actions:\n{corrective_str}\n"
            f"\nPreventive actions:\n{preventive_str}"
        )
Action Item Tracking Across Retrospective Formats

The four retrospective formats produce action items at different cadences, and without a unified tracking model, items from monthly deep dives will conflict with items from sprint retrospectives on the same topic. The improvement backlog acts as the single integration point: every action item from every retrospective format is written into the backlog with a source format tag, an owner, and a target date. The sprint team picks from this backlog during sprint planning, ensuring that retrospective actions compete with feature work on the same prioritised list rather than living in a separate document that no one reads.
Summary

Claude delivery retrospectives require four formats operating at different cadences, each tuned to the failure modes of AI-integrated systems. Sprint retrospectives surface operational friction within weeks. Quality deep dives identify systematic output quality patterns with a month of data. Architecture retrospectives evaluate whether long-term structural decisions have held up in practice. Incident retrospectives extract maximum learning from high-severity events within days. All four formats feed a unified improvement backlog where action items compete with feature work, ensuring that retrospective learnings are actioned rather than filed.

Add Claude-specific categories to every retrospective format: prompt quality, eval coverage, model behaviour, and integration are the four root-cause domains that standard agile formats do not surface.
Begin every quality deep dive with a prepared metrics report, not with a narrative discussion; data should direct the root-cause investigation, not confirm a narrative.
Run incident retrospectives within five days; the attribution step (prompt version and model version at incident time) must be completed before the retrospective, not reconstructed during it.
Track all retrospective action items in the same prioritised backlog as feature work; a separate 'retro tracking sheet' means items will not get resourced.
Review the improvement backlog health metric at every architecture retrospective to ensure the four-format system is producing closed items, not just generating work.
